overall_summary: ACC supports the ANOPR's balance between meeting AI-driven electricity demand growth and protecting ratepayers. The organization advocates for technology-neutral rules on load flexibility and co-location, cost responsibility that reflects actual load impact rather than peak demand, and transmission/generation expansion alongside near-term solutions like curtailment.

quotes.text: if large loads can commit to just a 1% curtailment rate, equivalent to only a few offline days per year, it could unlock around 126 GW of additional capacity on the grid
quotes.concern: Load flexibility via curtailment can deliver near-term grid relief

quotes.text: Maintain technology-neutral rules that are flexible and allow a variety of approaches and technologies to meet curtailment or hybrid-facility requirements.
quotes.concern: Rules should not prescribe specific technologies for meeting flexibility goals

quotes.text: Recognize the value of co-locating generation or storage both behind the meter and near or adjacent to the meter, to ensure no potential solutions are excluded.
quotes.concern: Co-location at multiple proximity levels should be explicitly recognized

quotes.text: The final rule should honor pre-existing agreements under state-regulated tariffs to maintain regulatory certainty and protect prior investments.
quotes.concern: Grandfathering needed to protect pre-rule interconnection agreements

quotes.text: Shifting the burden of network upgrades from ratepayers to large load project developers, largely driving current demand growth, could help alleviate near-term price surges for the broader public.
quotes.concern: Large loads should bear upgrade costs they directly cause

quotes.text: ensure that cost responsibility reflects a load's real-world impact, including any curtailment commitments or co-located generation and storage
quotes.concern: Cost studies must account for flexibility and co-located resources, not assume peak draw

quotes.text: demand response alone cannot replace the need for substantial new generation capacity, and that large loads should ideally be bringing their own generation online when they interconnect
quotes.concern: Flexibility is a complement, not substitute, for new generation

quotes.text: the grid will need to at least double in capacity to maintain affordability and reliability by mid-century
quotes.concern: Long-term infrastructure expansion is essential for cost and reliability

quotes.text: Building additional generation across all resource types—such as nuclear, natural gas, solar, and geothermal—along with expanded transmission, will provide the backbone of a reliable and affordable grid
quotes.concern: Multi-technology generation mix needed alongside transmission buildout

quotes.text: streamlining the siting and permitting of high-voltage, interregional transmission lines and generation resources, must proceed alongside near-term solutions, like those proposed in this ANOPR
quotes.concern: Permitting acceleration for transmission and generation should accompany near-term rules

bins.name: Cost allocation to large loads
bins.description: ACC supports shifting upgrade costs to large load developers proportional to their actual impact, not peak demand assumptions. This protects ratepayers from subsidizing new high-demand users while incentivizing load flexibility and co-located resources.

bins.name: Technology-neutral flexibility standards
bins.description: ACC advocates for rules that allow any technology or approach to meet curtailment and flexibility goals rather than prescribing specific solutions. This enables developers to choose the most effective methods for their projects.

bins.name: Co-location of generation and storage
bins.description: ACC supports explicit recognition of generation and storage co-located at multiple proximities (behind meter, near meter, adjacent to meter) to maximize developer options for grid relief.

bins.name: Proximity flexibility for co-located resources
bins.description: ACC emphasizes that co-located resources should be permitted at all viable proximity levels to ensure no solutions are excluded and developers can deploy resources as conditions allow.

bins.name: Near-term permitting and grid relief
bins.description: ACC sees load flexibility and permitting streamlining as urgent near-term solutions to grid stress, with curtailment alone potentially unlocking 126 GW of additional capacity while long-term infrastructure builds.

bins.name: Cost study methodology adjustments
bins.description: ACC argues interconnection studies should reflect actual load profiles and flexibility commitments rather than assuming peak draw at all times, ensuring cost allocations are fair and accurate.

bins.name: Load flexibility as grid resource
bins.description: ACC characterizes load flexibility and curtailment as valuable system resources that can deliver near-term grid relief alongside long-term infrastructure investment, citing data on capacity unlocked by modest curtailment rates.

bins.name: Technology-neutral approach
bins.description: ACC emphasizes that rules should not prescribe specific curtailment or hybrid technologies, instead allowing developers flexibility to choose forecasting, storage, or load management as conditions warrant.

bins.name: Pre-rule agreement grandfathering
bins.description: ACC calls for the final rule to honor pre-existing state-regulated tariff agreements to maintain regulatory certainty and protect prior investments from retroactive penalty.

bins.name: Load must pair with generation
bins.description: ACC cites independent market monitor findings that demand response alone cannot replace new generation capacity and that large loads should bring their own generation online at interconnection.

bins.name: Long-term grid expansion imperative
bins.description: ACC emphasizes that grid capacity must at least double by mid-century for affordability and reliability, with transmission expansion providing billions in economic benefits and supporting AI growth.

bins.name: Diverse generation expansion
bins.description: ACC supports expanding generation capacity across multiple resource types including nuclear, natural gas, solar, and geothermal alongside transmission expansion to build a reliable and affordable grid.

bins.name: Permitting streamlining for infrastructure
bins.description: ACC calls for streamlined siting and permitting of interregional transmission and generation resources to proceed alongside near-term solutions like those in the ANOPR.
